([Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language"): 省; [pinyin](/wiki/Pinyin "Pinyin"): *Shěng*) are the most numerous type of [province-level divisions](/wiki/Province-level_divisions_of_China "Province-level divisions of China"), the first-level subdivisions of the [People's Republic of China](/wiki/China "China") (PRC). There are currently 22 provinces administered by the PRC, and one further province that is claimed but not administered, which is [Taiwan](/wiki/Taiwan_Province,_People%27s_Republic_of_China "Taiwan Province, People's Republic of China").

**Provinces** ([Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language"): 省; [pinyin](/wiki/Pinyin "Pinyin"): *Shěng*) are the most numerous type of [province-level divisions](/wiki/Province-level_divisions_of_China "Province-level divisions of China"), the first-level subdivisions of the [People's Republic of China](/wiki/China "China") (PRC). There are currently 22 provinces administered by the PRC, and one further province that is claimed but not administered, which is [Taiwan](/wiki/Taiwan_Province,_People%27s_Republic_of_China "Taiwan Province, People's Republic of China").

The local governments of Chinese provinces consist of a Provincial People's Government headed by a [governor](/wiki/Governor_(China) "Governor (China)") that acts as the executive, a Provincial People's Congress with legislative powers, and a parallel provincial branch of the [Chinese Communist Party](/wiki/Chinese_Communist_Party "Chinese Communist Party") (CCP) that elects a [party secretary](/wiki/Chinese_Communist_Party_Committee_Secretary "Chinese Communist Party Committee Secretary") and a [provincial standing committee](/wiki/Chinese_Communist_Party_Provincial_Standing_Committee "Chinese Communist Party Provincial Standing Committee").

## Government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Provinces_of_China&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Government")]

Provinces are the most common form of province-level governments. The legislative bodies of the provinces are the Provincial People's Congresses. The executive branch is the Provincial People's Government, led by a [governor](/wiki/Governor_(China) "Governor (China)"). The People's Government is answerable to both the State Council and the Provincial People's Congress. The provincial branch of the CPC has a Provincial Party Congress every five years, and elects a [Standing Committee](/wiki/Chinese_Communist_Party_Provincial_Standing_Committee "Chinese Communist Party Provincial Standing Committee") to exercise its authority when not in session. The Provincial Party Secretary is the *de facto* most important position in the province.[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGoodman201596-1)[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESaich2015157–158-2)[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChungLam2010Chapter_2-3)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Provinces_of_China&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of the administrative divisions of China](/wiki/History_of_the_administrative_divisions_of_China "History of the administrative divisions of China")

The first provinces were created in the [Yuan dynasty](/wiki/Yuan_dynasty "Yuan dynasty"), and have remained one of the most stable forms of Chinese government since then.[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGuo201723-4) They were created to help the Imperial court manage local county governments, which were too numerous and far-flung to be managed directly.[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFitzgerald200216-5) The number of provinces grew steadily during subsequent dynasties, reaching 28 by the time of the [Republic of China](/wiki/Republic_of_China_(1912%E2%80%931949) "Republic of China (1912–1949)").[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGoodman2015150,_154-6) During the [Warlord Era](/wiki/Warlord_Era "Warlord Era"), provinces became largely or completely autonomous and exercised significant national influence. Province-level units proliferated and under the early People's Republic there were over 50.[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGoodman2015153–154-7)
Political boundaries are, in part, established to counterbalance the influence of economic factors. For instance, the [Yangtze Delta](/wiki/Yangtze_Delta "Yangtze Delta") is divided among the provinces of [Zhejiang](/wiki/Zhejiang "Zhejiang"), [Jiangsu](/wiki/Jiangsu "Jiangsu"), and [Anhui](/wiki/Anhui "Anhui"). This division ensures that economic strength is distributed, preventing any single region from potentially overpowering the state.[[8]](#cite_note-8)
